paper_title: Beyond Correctness: Evaluating Subjective Writing Preferences Across Cultures

dataset.name: WritingPreferenceBench
dataset.description: A cross-lingual dataset of 1,800 human-annotated preference pairs designed to isolate subjective writing quality by neutralizing objective confounds.

key_metrics.performance_gap: 29-percentage-point gap between generative reward models and other architectures.

key_findings: Sequence-based and zero-shot models perform at near-random levels on subjective preference tasks.
Generative reward models with explicit reasoning chains achieve significantly higher accuracy (81.8%).
All evaluated architectures exhibit severe genre instability (mean standard deviation 10.9%).
Performance does not consistently improve with model scale (e.g., 27B vs 8B).

benchmark_limitations_identified: WritingBench mixes creative with functional tasks.
LitBench uses Reddit upvotes as quality proxies.
Existing benchmarks predominantly focus on English, leaving cross-lingual preference evaluation largely unexplored.

title: Beyond Correctness: Evaluating Subjective Writing Preferences Across Cultures
